{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Should I use V-Sync with G-Sync/FreeSync?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "In NVIDIA Control Panel: Turn ON (acts as frame cap at top of VRR range) In-game: Turn OFF (prevents double-buffering lag) This combination gives you the best of both worlds: tear-free gaming within VRR range and smooth capping at max refresh."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What's the best response time setting?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Start at Medium/Normal, then test with testufo.com/ghosting. Increase if you see ghosting trails. Decrease if you see inverse ghosting (bright coronas). The \"1ms\" advertised often requires extreme overdrive that causes issues."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Should I use fullscreen or borderless window?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Competitive games: Exclusive fullscreen (lowest input lag) Casual/multitasking: Borderless windowed (alt-tab faster) Note: Windows 11 optimizations have reduced the difference significantly."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How do I know if my settings are working?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Use these tests: - Refresh rate: testufo.com (shows actual Hz) - G-Sync/FreeSync: NVIDIA Pendulum or VRR indicator in monitor OSD - Response time: testufo.com/ghosting - Input lag: Feel for responsiveness, or use NVIDIA Reflex"
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is HDR worth enabling?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes if: - Your monitor is HDR600 or better - Game has good HDR support - You've calibrated HDR settings No if: - Monitor is only HDR400 - HDR looks washed out - Game has poor HDR implementation ---"
      }
    },
    {

config.faq_structured_data:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How do I know what resolution my monitor supports?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Check your monitor's specifications in the manual or manufacturer website. Most monitors display their native resolution in the product name (e.g., \"24-inch 1080p Monitor\"). You can also check in Windows Display Settings or Mac System Preferences where all supported resolutions are listed in the dropdown menu."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What's the difference between resolution and screen size?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Resolution is the number of pixels (e.g., 1920×1080), while screen size is the physical diagonal measurement in inches (e.g., 24\"). The same resolution can appear on different sized screens - a 1080p laptop screen looks much sharper than a 1080p TV because the pixels are packed more densely (higher PPI)."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Should I use the highest resolution my monitor supports?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Generally yes, use your monitor's native resolution for the sharpest image. However, consider performance: higher resolutions require more GPU power for gaming and may make text/UI elements very small, requiring scaling adjustments for comfortable viewing."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Why does my 4K monitor look blurry on Windows?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "This is usually due to scaling issues. 4K monitors at 100% scaling make everything tiny, so Windows automatically scales to 150-200%. Some older applications don't handle scaling well. Try adjusting scaling in Display Settings or enable \"Fix scaling for apps\" in Windows 10/11."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can I use different resolutions on multiple monitors?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, modern operating systems handle different resolutions well. However, mouse movement between screens might feel inconsistent, and window dragging can be awkward if resolutions differ significantly. For best experience, try to match resolutions or at least aspect ratios."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What resolution should I use for streaming or recording?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "For streaming: 1080p 60fps is the most common. Use 720p 60fps for slower internet or 1440p if you have excellent upload speed. For recording: Use your native resolution for best quality, or 1080p for broader compatibility and smaller file sizes."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How do I change my resolution if I can't see the screen properly?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Boot into Safe Mode (Windows) or Recovery Mode (Mac) where lower resolutions are used by default. On Windows, you can also press Windows + P to cycle through display modes, or use Windows + I to open Settings and navigate to Display by keyboard. On Mac, reset NVRAM/PRAM by holding Option + Command + P + R during startup."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is higher resolution always better?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Not necessarily. Higher resolution provides more detail and screen space but requires more GPU power, makes text smaller (requiring scaling), and creates larger file sizes for content creation. The \"best\" resolution depends on your screen size, use case, graphics hardware, and viewing distance. ---"
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",

      "acceptedAnswer": {

The \"best\" resolution depends on your screen size, use case, graphics hardware, and viewing distance. ---"
      }
    }
  ]
}

config.faq_structured_data:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What is the most common laptop screen size?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "15.6 inches is the most common laptop screen size globally, accounting for approximately 40-50% of laptop sales. It offers the best balance between screen real estate, portability, cost, and component availability. The second most popular is 13.3-14 inches for ultraportable laptops."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How do I measure a laptop screen with a broken screen?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "If the screen is broken but display boundaries are still visible, measure the diagonal of the visible display area (not the bezel). If completely shattered, check your laptop model number on the bottom sticker or search online for your model's specifications. The screen size is almost always listed in official specs."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Does laptop screen size include the bezel?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "No, laptop screen size does not include the bezel (frame). It only measures the active display area - the diagonal distance of the actual screen. This is why two laptops with the same screen size (e.g., 15.6\") can have different overall dimensions if one has thinner bezels."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can I replace my laptop screen with a different size?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Generally no. Laptop screens are specifically designed for each model. The screen size, mounting points, connector type, and cable length are model-specific. However, you can sometimes find screens with the same physical size but different resolution or panel type (IPS vs. TN) that are compatible. Always verify compatibility before purchasing."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What's the difference between laptop screen size and resolution?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Screen size is the physical diagonal measurement in inches (e.g., 15.6\"). Resolution is the number of pixels (e.g., 1920×1080). They're independent - the same 15.6\" screen can have 1366×768 (HD), 1920×1080 (Full HD), or 3840×2160 (4K) resolution. Higher resolution means sharper image but requires more GPU power."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is a 15.6\" laptop too big to carry?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "It depends on your needs and tolerance. A 15.6\" laptop typically weighs 4-5.5 lbs (1.8-2.5 kg) and fits in most backpacks. For daily commuting or frequent travel, 13-14\" is generally more comfortable. For occasional carry between home and office, 15.6\" is manageable. Consider a lightweight 15.6\" model (under 4 lbs) if you need the screen space but want better portability."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How do I measure a MacBook screen?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Measure exactly the same way as any laptop: diagonal distance from corner to corner of the active display area only, excluding the black bezel. MacBooks make this easy because the aluminum bezel is clearly distinct from the glass display. For recent models, the size is in the product name (MacBook Pro 14-inch, 16-inch, MacBook Air 13-inch)."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What laptop screen size is best for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "15.6\" to 17.3\" is ideal for gaming laptops. 15.6\" offers the best balance - still somewhat portable, fits gaming hardware, large enough for immersion. 17.3\" provides maximum immersion but sacrifices portability. Screens smaller than 15\" often can't fit high-performance gaming components. Also consider high refresh rate (144Hz+) which is more important than size for competitive gaming. ---"
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What laptop screen size is best for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "15.6\" to 17.3\" is ideal for gaming laptops. 15.6\" offers the best balance - still somewhat portable, fits gaming hardware, large enough for immersion. 17.3\" provides maximum immersion but sacrifices portability. Screens smaller than 15\" often can't fit high-performance gaming components. Also consider high refresh rate (144Hz+) which is more important than size for competitive gaming. ---"
      }
    }
  ]
}
</script>

config.faq_structured_data:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Do I measure the screen or the bezel?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Only measure the screen, not the bezel (frame). The advertised monitor size refers only to the visible display area, excluding the plastic or metal frame around it."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is monitor size measured diagonally?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, always diagonally, from one corner of the screen to the diagonally opposite corner. This is the industry standard for all monitors and TVs."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How do I measure a curved monitor?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Measure the straight-line diagonal distance, not following the curve. Use a ruler or tape measure and measure as if the screen were flat, from corner to corner in a straight line."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What if my measurement is between standard sizes?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Round to the nearest standard size. For example: - 23.5\"-23.9\" = 24\" monitor - 27.5\"-28\" = 27\" monitor - 31.5\"-32.5\" = 32\" monitor Manufacturers sometimes specify 23.8\" or 27.5\", but these are marketed as 24\" and 27\" respectively."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can I use my phone to measure monitor size?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, if you have a measuring app that uses your phone's camera and AR capabilities. Apps like \"Measure\" (iOS) or \"Measure\" (Android) can estimate dimensions. However, physical measurement is more accurate."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How do I measure an ultrawide monitor?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Same method: Measure diagonally from corner to corner. Ultrawide monitors have larger diagonal measurements (34\", 38\", 49\") but the measurement technique is identical."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What's the difference between monitor size and screen resolution?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Monitor size: Physical diagonal measurement (e.g., 27\") Screen resolution: Number of pixels (e.g., 2560×1440) They're independent: - Same size can have different resolutions - Same resolution can appear on different sizes"
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How accurate does my measurement need to be?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Within ±0.5 inch is sufficient for most purposes. Manufacturers themselves have small variations (a \"27 inch\" might be 27.0\" or 27.5\"). Round to the nearest standard size for practical purposes. ---"
      }
    },
    {

config.faq_structured_data:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What size monitor should I buy for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "For most gamers, 27 inches at 1440p is the sweet spot. It offers enough screen real estate for immersion without being too large to see the entire screen comfortably. For competitive esports, some prefer 24 inches at 1080p to see everything at a glance. If you sit further back or want maximum immersion, 32 inches at 4K works well."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is 4K worth it in 2025?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "It depends on your use case. For content creation, photo editing, and productivity with lots of text, 4K is excellent at 27\" or larger. For gaming, 4K requires a powerful GPU (RTX 4070 or better) to run smoothly at high refresh rates. For casual use and gaming on mid-range hardware, 1440p offers better value."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "IPS or VA for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "IPS is generally better for most gaming due to faster response times and better color accuracy. Choose VA if you primarily play single-player games, watch movies, or game in a dark room where the superior contrast is noticeable. Avoid VA for competitive multiplayer due to potential black smearing."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How much should I spend on a monitor?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "A good rule is to spend 15-25% of your total PC budget on the monitor. For a $1000 PC, budget $150-250. For a $2000 PC, budget $300-500. Remember monitors last longer than other components, so it's worth investing in quality."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Do I need G-Sync or FreeSync?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "FreeSync is free and works with both AMD and NVIDIA GPUs, making it the practical choice for most buyers. Hardware G-Sync (with the module) offers slightly better performance at the extreme low end of the refresh range, but the premium is only worth it for high-end setups with NVIDIA GPUs."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What's the best refresh rate for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "144Hz is the sweet spot for most gamers - it's a huge improvement over 60Hz and achievable with mid-range GPUs. 240Hz offers diminishing returns unless you play competitive esports. 60Hz is only acceptable for single-player games or if you have a very weak GPU."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Should I buy a curved monitor?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Curved monitors are personal preference. They can provide a more immersive experience for gaming and help reduce eye strain on ultrawide displays. However, they're not ideal for professional work requiring straight lines (CAD, graphic design). For standard 16:9 monitors, flat is usually fine."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How long do monitors last?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Quality monitors typically last 5-10 years. LED backlights may dim over time, but modern monitors rarely fail outright. OLED panels may degrade faster due to organic materials, though this takes many years of heavy use. Buy quality and your monitor will outlast several PC upgrades. ---"
      }
    },
    {

      }

<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Faut-il utiliser V-Sync avec G-Sync/FreeSync ?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Dans le panneau NVIDIA : Activez (agit comme plafond d'images en haut de la plage VRR) Dans les jeux : Désactivez (évite le lag du double buffering) Cette combinaison donne le meilleur des deux mondes : pas de tearing dans la plage VRR et un plafonnement fluide au taux max."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Quel réglage de temps de réponse choisir ?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Commencez à Moyen/Normal, puis testez avec testufo.com/ghosting. Augmentez si vous voyez des traînées. Diminuez si vous voyez du ghosting inversé (halos lumineux). Le \"1ms\" annoncé demande souvent un overdrive extrême qui cause des problèmes."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Plein écran ou fenêtré sans bordure ?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Jeux compétitifs : Plein écran exclusif (input lag le plus bas) Casual/multitâche : Fenêtré sans bordure (alt-tab plus rapide) Note : Les optimisations de Windows 11 ont beaucoup réduit la différence."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Le HDR vaut-il le coup ?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Oui si : - Votre écran est HDR600 ou mieux - Le jeu a un bon support HDR - Vous avez calibré les réglages HDR Non si : - L'écran est seulement HDR400 - Le HDR paraît délavé - Le jeu a une mauvaise implémentation HDR ---"
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Le HDR vaut-il le coup ?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Oui si : - Votre écran est HDR600 ou mieux - Le jeu a un bon support HDR - Vous avez calibré les réglages HDR Non si : - L'écran est seulement HDR400 - Le HDR paraît délavé - Le jeu a une mauvaise implémentation HDR ---"
      }
    }
  ]
}
</script>

<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is 1440p worth it over 1080p for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, if you have at least an RTX 4060 Ti or RX 7700 XT and primarily play AAA games. The 33% increase in sharpness on a 27\" monitor is significant, and modern GPUs can maintain 100+ FPS in most titles."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How much FPS do you lose going from 1080p to 1440p?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Approximately 40 50% FPS loss at 1440p compared to 1080p on the same GPU and settings. A game running at 200 FPS at 1080p will run around 110 120 FPS at 1440p."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can you tell the difference between 1080p and 1440p?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, definitely —especially on 27\" monitors. Text, UI elements, and distant details are noticeably sharper at 1440p. On 24\" monitors, the difference is less pronounced but still visible."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What GPU do I need for 1440p 144Hz gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Minimum : RTX 4060 Ti or RX 7700 XT (100 120 FPS in most games) Recommended : RTX 4070 or RX 7800 XT (120 144 FPS in most games) Ideal : RTX 4070 Ti or better (144+ FPS in all games)"
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Should I get 1080p 240Hz or 1440p 144Hz?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "1080p 240Hz : If you play competitive FPS and want maximum performance 1440p 144Hz : If you play a variety of games and value visual quality Budget consideration : They cost about the same (~$1,500 1,800 total build), so choose based on gaming preference, not budget."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is 1080p still good in 2025?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Absolutely yes . 1080p remains the competitive gaming standard, offers excellent value, and modern games still look great at this resolution with high settings. Over 60% of PC gamers still use 1080p as their primary resolution."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can a GTX 1660 handle 1440p gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "For older/less demanding games at medium settings, yes. For modern AAA games, you'll struggle to maintain 60 FPS. A GTX 1660 is best paired with 1080p gaming.       }
    }
  ]
}
</script>

      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is G-Sync worth the extra cost?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Rarely in 2025. The $100 500 G Sync premium is only justifiable for enthusiasts seeking G Sync Ultimate features (premium HDR, ULMB) or users who specifically need the best possible low framerate performance. For 90% of gamers, FreeSync Premium provides equivalent experience at much lower cost."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can you use FreeSync with AMD and G-Sync with NVIDIA?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "FreeSync works with both AMD (native) and NVIDIA (G Sync Compatible) GPUs. G Sync only works with NVIDIA GPUs. This makes FreeSync the more flexible choice for users who might change GPU brands in the future."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What VRR range should I look for?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Look for monitors with at least 48 144Hz range with LFC (Low Framerate Compensation). FreeSync Premium certification guarantees this. Avoid basic FreeSync monitors with narrow ranges (e.g., 48 75Hz) as they provide poor low framerate experience."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Do consoles support G-Sync or FreeSync?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "PS5 and Xbox Series X support HDMI 2.1 VRR, which is compatible with FreeSync but not G Sync. If you game on consoles, choose a FreeSync monitor with HDMI 2.1 support. G Sync monitors won't provide VRR functionality with consoles."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Should I disable VSync when using VRR?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, always disable V Sync when using G Sync or FreeSync. VRR replaces V Sync functionality while providing lower input lag. Enabling both simultaneously can cause conflicts and reduce performance."
      }
    },

      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Does VRR affect image quality?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {

  ]
}
</script>

config.faq_structured_data:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is a gaming monitor better than a TV for PS5?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "For competitive gaming, yes—monitors have lower input lag (1 3ms vs 5 10ms) and faster response times. For single player and immersive gaming, TVs are better—larger screen (55\"+), superior HDR, and more comfortable couch gaming experience. Most PS5 users prefer TVs for the living room setup, but competitive players choose monitors."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can I use a TV as a PC monitor?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, but with considerations. Modern 4K TVs work well as PC monitors for productivity and gaming. However: (1) TVs are large (43\"+) requiring more desk space, (2) text clarity may be lower than native monitors at close viewing, (3) some TVs have chroma subsampling issues in PC mode. Best for couch gaming, not ideal for desk work."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What size monitor is best for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "24\" for competitive esports (1080p 144 240Hz), 27\" for balanced gaming (1440p 144 165Hz), 32\" for immersive gaming (1440p/4K). Larger than 32\" becomes unwieldy for desk use. Professional gamers prefer 24 27\" for better visual tracking of entire screen without head movement."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Do gaming TVs have higher input lag than monitors?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, but the gap has narrowed. Best gaming monitors: 1 3ms input lag. Best gaming TVs: 5 10ms input lag. The 4 7ms difference matters in competitive esports but is negligible in casual gaming. Budget TVs (30ms+) still have poor input lag—avoid for gaming."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is OLED or QLED better for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "OLED is better for image quality (perfect blacks, infinite contrast, instant response time) but has burn in risk and costs more. QLED is brighter (better for bright rooms), no burn in risk, and usually cheaper. For gaming: OLED for best experience, QLED for better value and peace of mind. Both are good choices in 2025."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can a 60Hz TV be good for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "For single player and casual gaming, yes—60Hz is adequate. For competitive gaming, no—60Hz feels sluggish compared to 120Hz+ after acclimation. PS5 and Xbox Series X support 120Hz in many games, making 60Hz TVs a bottleneck. Minimum recommendation: 120Hz for modern console gaming."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Why do pros use gaming monitors instead of TVs?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Four reasons: (1) Lower input lag (1 3ms vs 5 10ms) for faster reactions, (2) Higher refresh rates (240 360Hz vs max 120Hz TV), (3) Smaller size (24 27\") allows tracking entire screen without eye movement, (4) Desk setup is tournament standard. No competitive disadvantages tolerated at pro level."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is a curved monitor or TV better for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Curved monitors (1800R 1000R) provide more immersive experience for desk gaming, reduce eye strain, and improve viewing angles. Most beneficial at 27\"+ and ultrawide formats. Curved TVs are rare and unnecessary—viewing distance too great for curve to matter. For desk gaming: curved is nice bonus. For living room: flat is fine."
      }
    }
  ]
}
</script>

config.faq_structured_data:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is HDR worth it for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, but only with proper HDR monitors (HDR1000+ or OLED). True HDR gaming provides significantly better contrast, more realistic lighting, and enhanced immersion in supported games. However, avoid fake HDR (HDR400/600) monitors as they provide minimal benefit over good SDR displays."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What's the difference between HDR400 and HDR1000?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "HDR400 is \"fake HDR\" with insufficient brightness (400 nits) and no local dimming, providing minimal improvement over SDR. HDR1000 requires 1000 nits peak brightness and local dimming (≥384 zones), delivering true HDR experience with deeper blacks and brighter highlights simultaneously."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Do I need a special GPU for HDR gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Most modern GPUs support HDR (GTX 1000+ series, RX 500+ series). However, HDR gaming requires ~10 15% more GPU power due to increased bandwidth and processing requirements. For 1440p HDR gaming, RTX 3060+ or RX 6600 XT+ recommended."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can I use HDR for competitive gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "HDR can work for competitive gaming, but many pros avoid it due to potential input lag, inconsistent brightness, and non standard appearance. If using HDR competitively, ensure your monitor has <5ms input lag in HDR mode and practice extensively with HDR enabled."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Should I buy OLED or Mini-LED for HDR gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "OLED provides better HDR experience (perfect blacks, infinite contrast) but has burn in risk and costs more. Mini LED offers good HDR with no burn in risk and better brightness, but with some blooming. For mixed gaming/productivity: Mini LED. For pure gaming experience: OLED."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Why does HDR look washed out in some games?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Poor HDR implementation, incorrect monitor settings, or fake HDR monitors cause washed out appearance. Ensure you have true HDR monitor (HDR1000+), configure peak brightness correctly in game, and use monitors with proper local dimming. Some games have poor HDR implementation regardless of hardware."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is Windows HDR mode necessary for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, Windows HDR mode must be enabled for HDR gaming, but it often makes desktop content look washed out. Best practice: enable HDR only when gaming, disable for regular desktop use. Windows 11 has improved HDR handling compared to Windows 10."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "How much should I spend on an HDR gaming monitor?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "For true HDR gaming: minimum $800 1000 (entry OLED or basic Mini LED). Budget $1200 2000 for excellent HDR experience. Below $800, focus on high quality SDR monitor instead of fake HDR. Don't pay premium for HDR400/600 certification."
      }
    }
  ]
}
</script>

config.faq_structured_data:
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "FAQPage",
  "mainEntity": [
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is IPS or TN better for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "IPS is better for most gamers in 2025. Modern Fast IPS offers 1 2ms response times (nearly matching TN) with vastly superior color accuracy and viewing angles. TN is only better if you need 360Hz+ on a strict budget. 59% of professional gamers now use IPS vs 36% TN."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What is the best panel type for competitive gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Fast IPS (1 2ms G2G) at 240Hz+ is optimal for competitive gaming in 2025. It balances TN like speed with better colors and no viewing angle issues. OLED is even better but expensive. TN remains viable on budget but image quality suffers significantly."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is VA good for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "VA is good for single player immersive gaming (horror, RPGs, space sims) where contrast matters more than speed. VA is poor for competitive or fast paced gaming due to slow response times (4 8ms) and black smearing. Not recommended for FPS, racing, or action games."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Do I need OLED for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "No, OLED is not needed but offers the ultimate gaming experience with perfect blacks, instant response, and vibrant colors. IPS provides 85 90% of OLED quality at half the price without burn in concerns. OLED is worth it for enthusiasts who can afford $1000+ and will use varied content to avoid burn in."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What is IPS glow and is it a problem?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "IPS glow is light leakage visible in screen corners when viewing dark content in dark rooms. It varies by unit quality and viewing angle. Modern IPS has reduced this issue significantly. It's noticeable but usually minor annoyance, not a deal breaker for most users. OLED eliminates it completely."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can you see the difference between 1ms and 4ms response time?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Most gamers cannot clearly distinguish 1ms from 4ms response time in typical gaming. The difference becomes noticeable above 5 6ms as motion blur increases. Professional competitive players may notice 2 3ms differences. For most gamers, 1 4ms is \"fast enough\" if other monitor qualities (color, refresh rate) are good."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is TN dead for gaming monitors?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Not dead but declining. TN retains advantages in extreme budget 240Hz+ monitors and 360 500Hz esports displays. However, Fast IPS has largely replaced TN in mainstream gaming. Major manufacturers are reducing TN models. By 2026 2027, TN will likely be niche/legacy technology except at extreme refresh rates."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What panel does my favorite streamer use?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Most popular streamers use IPS or OLED panels because they look better on camera and provide better image quality for viewers. While some competitive streamers use TN for performance, IPS is overwhelmingly preferred for streaming due to superior color reproduction that translates better to video encoding."
      }
    }
  ]
}
</script>

      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Is ultrawide better than dual monitors for gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "For immersive single player gaming, yes—ultrawide provides seamless, cinematic experience without bezels. For competitive gaming and multitasking, dual monitors are better due to 16:9 compatibility, secondary screen for Discord/guides, and higher refresh rate availability. 85% of games support ultrawide, but competitive titles often don't."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can I use an ultrawide monitor for competitive FPS games?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Most competitive FPS games either don't support ultrawide (CS2, Valorant) or the extra FOV is considered a disadvantage. 99% of professional players use standard 16:9 monitors. Ultrawides are tournament banned. For casual competitive play, some games like COD and Apex work, but you may be at a disadvantage."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "What GPU do I need for ultrawide gaming?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "For 3440×1440 (21:9) at 60 FPS: RTX 4060 Ti minimum. For 144 FPS: RTX 4070 or better. For 5120×1440 (32:9) at 60 FPS: RTX 4070. For 144 FPS: RTX 4080 or 4090. Ultrawide requires 34 100% more GPU power than standard 1440p."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Are ultrawide monitors worth it in 2025?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, if you primarily play single player games, do content creation, or value immersive experiences. Not worth it if you play competitive FPS, stream regularly, or need true multitasking. Consider your primary use case—ultrawides excel at specific tasks but aren't universally better."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Should I get a 34\" ultrawide or dual 27\" monitors?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "34\" ultrawide if: immersive gaming, video editing, programming, clean aesthetics. Dual 27\" if: competitive gaming, streaming, multitasking, flexibility. Budget: dual 27\" can be cheaper ($700 vs $500 800). Performance: ultrawide needs better GPU."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Do all games support ultrawide monitors?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "No. About 85% of modern AAA games fully support 21:9. Most competitive FPS don't (CS2, Valorant locked to 16:9). 32:9 support is ~45%. Older games and some console ports lack support. Community mods exist but aren't recommended for online games."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Can I use a 49\" ultrawide as two monitors?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Yes, most 49\" ultrawide monitors have Picture by Picture (PbP) mode that treats them as two separate displays. However, you'll have a bezel free split in the middle. Better than physical bezel but not true dual monitors. Windows treats it as one or two displays depending on mode."
      }
    },
    {
      "@type": "Question",
      "name": "Which is better for productivity: ultrawide or dual monitors?",
      "acceptedAnswer": {
        "@type": "Answer",
        "text": "Depends on workflow. Ultrawide better for: coding, video editing, seamless workflows, clean aesthetics. Dual monitors better for: video calls + work, completely separate applications, mixed orientations (landscape + portrait), window management. Most productive: 49\" ultrawide or triple monitors, but overkill for many."
      }
    }
  ]
}
